perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that it will take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures shall include the highest degree of care that Recipient utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that it will take measures it deems appropriate to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, with care levels that are considered adequate.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'all reasonable measures' with 'measures it deems appropriate' and 'highest degree of care' with 'care levels that are considered adequate,' introducing subjectivity and ambiguity. This weakens the obligation to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Regulations require reasonable steps to be taken to keep information secret. 'Measures deemed appropriate' and 'care levels considered adequate' do not necessarily equate to 'reasonable steps,' especially if the measures chosen are insufficient, undermining the requirements for protecting trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity that undermines the original obligation to protect confidential information. The original text mandates 'all reasonable measures' and 'the highest degree of care,' while the changed text uses 'measures it deems appropriate' and 'care levels that are considered adequate.' This change, as the Law Explanation points out, means the new standard doesn't necessarily equate to 'reasonable steps.' Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly helpful, the Law Explanation supports the idea that this change creates a significant ambiguity, weakening the protection of trade secrets and confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Recipient or its directors, officers, employees, consultants or agents are requested or required by legal process to disclose any of the Confident ial Information, the Recipient shall give prompt notice so that the Discloser may seek a protective order or other appropriate relief. In the event that such protective order is not obtained, the Recipient shall disclose only that portion of the Confidenti al Information which its counsel advises that it is legally required to disclose.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Recipient or its directors, officers, employees, consultants or agents are requested or required by legal process to disclose any of the Confidential Information, the Recipient will attempt to give notice so that the Discloser may consider seeking a protective order or other relief. The Recipient will disclose only that portion of the Confidential Information which its counsel suggests is necessary to disclose.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'shall give prompt notice' with 'will attempt to give notice,' making the notification discretionary. It also changes 'counsel advises that it is legally required to disclose' to 'counsel suggests is necessary to disclose,' reducing the legal obligation to disclose only what is absolutely required.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CPR requires parties to comply with court orders, including disclosure. By weakening the notification and disclosure obligation, this modification potentially contradicts the civil procedure rules and frustrates the process for requesting protective orders.

Instruments 1998 No. 3132 (L. 17) PART 31 Rule 31.17 Table of Contents Content More Resources Previous: Provision Next: Provision Plain View Print Options What Version Latest available (Revised) Original (As made) Opening Options Open whole Instrument Open Instrument without Schedules Open Schedules only More Resources Status: This is the original version (as it was originally made). Orders for disclosure against a person not a party 31.17 —(1) This rule applies where an application is made to the court under any Act for disclosure by a person who is not a party to the proceedings( 1 ). (2) The application must be supported by evidence. (3) The court may make an order under this rule only where— (a) the documents of which disclosure is sought are likely to support the case of the applicant or adversely affect the case of one of the other parties to the proceedings; and (b) disclosure is necessary in order to dispose fairly of the claim or to save costs. (4) An order under this rule must— (a) specify the documents or the classes of documents which the respondent must disclose; and (b) require the respondent, when making disclosure, to specify any of those documents— (i) which are no longer in his control; or (ii) in respect of which he claims a right or duty to withhold inspection. (5) Such an order may— (a) require the respondent to indicate what has happened to any documents which are no longer in his control; and (b) specify the time and place for disclosure and inspection. ( 1 ) An application for disclosure against a person who is not a party to proceedings is permitted under section 34 of the Supreme Court Act 1981(c. 54) or section 53 of the County Courts Act 1984 (c. 28) . perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to provide prompt notice and alters the standard for disclosure, potentially contradicting the requirement for compliance with court orders. The original text stated the Recipient 'shall give prompt notice,' creating a clear obligation. The changed text replaces this with 'will attempt to give notice,' introducing ambiguity and making the notification discretionary. This directly undermines the process of seeking protective orders, as the Discloser may not receive timely notification. Additionally, the original text required disclosure of only what counsel 'advises that it is legally required to disclose,' while the modified text changes this to what counsel 'suggests is necessary to disclose.' This lowers the threshold for disclosure and could lead to the disclosure of more information than legally required. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses orders for disclosure against a person who is not a party, and the importance of necessity for disclosure; this modification, however, may lead to unnecessary disclosure, or failing to provide appropriate notice which undermines legal proceedings.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of the United Kingdom, and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in United Kingdom and worldwide.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally interpreted under principles resembling the laws of the United Kingdom, and its applicability upon the parties will depend upon mutual understandings.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of the United Kingdom' with 'shall be generally interpreted under principles resembling the laws of the United Kingdom,' replacing a clear governing law with a vague resemblance. Also replacing 'shall be binding upon the parties' to 'will depend upon mutual understandings', which removes the guarantee of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Contracts (Applicable Law) Act 1990 implements the Rome Convention, requiring a clear choice of law for contractual obligations. Stating the agreement is 'generally interpreted under principles resembling the laws' rather than clearly governed by UK law creates ambiguity and contradicts the Act's requirement for a defined applicable law.

Words denoting number, gender, and so forth In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise- words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words "insane" and "insane person
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates adherence to UK law, whereas the changed text replaces this with a vague reference to 'principles resembling' UK law and replaces 'shall be binding upon the parties' to 'will depend upon mutual understandings'. The Law Explanation states that this change contradicts the Contracts (Applicable Law) Act 1990 (Rome Convention) which requires a clear choice of law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not contain relevant information to support or refute this claim.
